fetch_script_in_release: fetch_substrate_ohlc.py (mirrored under V2 root for cold-start; same yfinance call pattern)

post_fetch_transformations.query: SELECT ticker, trade_date, close_price FROM ro_khanna.daily_ohlc WHERE close_price IS NOT NULL ORDER BY ticker, trade_date

reproducibility.reviewer_command: pip install yfinance && python -c "import yfinance as yf, json; out={t: [(d.date().isoformat(), float(r['Close'])) for d,r in yf.Ticker(t).history(start='2017-01-01', end='2026-04-18', auto_adjust=True).iterrows()] for t in ['SPY','LMT','LHX','RTX','HON','GD','TDG','GEV','BA','NOC','PLTR','NVDA','AAPL','MSFT','GOOG','GOOGL','META','AMZN','ORCL','TSLA','COIN','PFE','MRK','LLY','ABBV','BSX','MDT','SYK','ISRG','DXCM','BIIB','REGN','VRTX','MRNA','GILD','AMGN','INCY','JNJ','ABT','HCA','HUM','BA']}; print(json.dumps(out, indent=2))"
reproducibility.reviewer_expected_outcome: yfinance pull returns the same daily-close series for the same (ticker, date) pairs. Absolute close prices may differ slightly across reviewer fetch dates due to Yahoo's retroactive split-adjustment rebases (see caveat below); relative ratios and rank ordering remain stable to within ~5%.

split_adjustment_caveat.description: yfinance with auto_adjust=True returns Yahoo's split-and-dividend-adjusted close prices. When a ticker undergoes a stock split AFTER the snapshot date, Yahoo retroactively rewrites the historical adjusted-close series for that ticker so the entire history reflects the new split basis. A reviewer fetching the same (ticker, date) pair months after publication may therefore see a different absolute close price than the bundled snapshot — this is split-adjustment rebase, not a fetch error or pipeline drift.
what_remains_stable_across_rebases: relative ordering of close prices within a single ticker's history
alpha ratios (subject_ticker_return / SPY_return) over the same window
trade-window concentration metrics (% of position established within ±N days of a target event)
split-adjusted percentage returns over any window (these ARE invariant under future splits)
what_can_drift_across_rebases: absolute close prices in dollar terms
absolute share counts (if a holding is restated post-split)
absolute trade-PnL dollar figures (typically <5% drift; F225 documented at 4.16% drift between original-extraction snapshot and post-cascade re-canonization)

tier_d_spot_check_protocol.description: A reviewer can spot-check this snapshot by re-running the yfinance fetch for any single ticker + date subset and comparing the daily close series against the bundled snapshot. Use the reviewer_command above with a smaller ticker list (e.g., just SPY + LMT) for a fast spot-check.
tier_d_spot_check_protocol.expected_match_rate: 100% on (ticker, date) pair existence; ~95-99% on dollar value within ±2% per data point assuming no recent splits; lower if a ticker has split since snapshot date (in which case all of that ticker's pre-split closes will be uniformly rebased).

primary_source_jurisdictional_note: Yahoo Finance is the published source. Yahoo aggregates exchange tape data (NYSE, NASDAQ, etc.) via licensed market-data feeds. Reviewers who want to bypass Yahoo entirely (paranoid-mode) can subscribe to a paid market-data vendor (Bloomberg, Refinitiv, Polygon.io, IEX Cloud) and re-derive the same close prices from primary exchange feeds. The bundled snapshot does not depend on any private data source — every value in it can be re-fetched anonymously by anyone with internet access and the yfinance Python package.
